sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Knee pain may relate to patellofemoral overload, ligament injury, meniscal symptoms, tendon pain, or fracture. Physio assessment should prioritise weight-bearing, instability, and effusion patterns.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain with stairs, kneeling, squatting, running, pivoting, or prolonged sitting
- Swelling, giving way, locking, or apprehension
- Reduced extension or flexion
- Limp or difficulty weight-bearing after acute injury

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Escalate for suspected fracture, dislocation, septic joint, acute extensor mechanism rupture, true locked knee, or major instability after trauma
- Rapid large effusion after twisting trauma raises concern for significant internal derangement
- Patellofemoral pain often reflects load sensitivity rather than structural catastrophe

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen trauma mechanism, immediate swelling, extension ability, and safe ambulation
- Provide support, unloading advice, and simple pain management within scope
- Escalate when the patient cannot actively extend, cannot weight-bear after trauma, or has marked swelling or deformity

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Perform focused assessment of ligament, meniscal, patellofemoral, and tendon features
- Begin graded loading, quadriceps and hip strength, and movement modification where appropriate
- Consider taping, brace review, or gait aid advice if it improves safe function
- Refer for imaging or specialist input when locking, gross instability, fracture concern, or poor progress persists

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Document whether swelling was immediate or delayed and whether the patient can fully extend the knee
